planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Franklinia is notoriously difficult to establish and demands excellent drainage — it will not tolerate wet, heavy clay or alkaline soil.

directions: Franklinia alatamaha is one of the most historically remarkable trees in cultivation: extinct in its native habitat along the Altamaha River in Georgia since 1803, every living tree on earth descends from seeds collected by John and William Bartram in 1765 and 1773. It is a spectacular small ornamental tree, producing large (3-inch), pure-white, five-petaled, fragrant flowers with golden centers from late summer through fall, often blooming simultaneously with its brilliant scarlet and orange autumn foliage in October — one of the most beautiful autumn displays of any small tree. Growing it successfully is a meaningful horticultural and conservation act, keeping this species alive in cultivation more than 200 years after its wild extinction.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in moist, well-drained, very slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade — virginia stewartia is notoriously difficult to establish and absolutely requires excellent drainage, consistently moist (not wet) soil, and protection from afternoon heat in zones 8–9; mulch heavily to maintain cool, moist root conditions.

directions: Stewartia malacodendron is the most beautiful and rarest of the native stewartias, a slow-growing, multi-stemmed shrub to small tree of open forest understories in the coastal plain from Virginia to Florida and Texas, producing extraordinarily showy, wide-open, camellia-like white flowers 3–4 inches across with distinctive, dramatic, purple-blue stamens and dark-purple anthers in June and July — the most spectacular flowers of any stewartia. It also has handsome, flaking, mottled gray-and-cinnamon bark and brilliant red autumn foliage. A coveted collector's plant for shaded native woodland gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; mountain stewartia is more adaptable than S. malacodendron but still demands excellent drainage, consistently moist soil, and protection from drying winds and hot afternoon sun in zone 7–9.

directions: Stewartia ovata is a large, multi-stemmed, native Appalachian shrub to small tree producing some of the largest flowers of any native deciduous plant — white, camellia-like, five-petaled blooms up to 4 inches across with large, showy orange stamens and dark-purple anthers in June and July — as well as outstanding, multi-season ornamental interest from its handsome, peeling gray-tan-cinnamon mottled bark and brilliant red, orange, and purple fall foliage. Native to moist mountain forest understories from Virginia to Alabama, it is the most widely adaptable stewartia for eastern gardens and is slowly becoming available from quality native plant nurseries.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; red bay is extremely drought-tolerant once established but is currently under severe threat from laurel wilt disease caused by a fungal pathogen (Raffaelea lauricola) introduced on ambrosia beetles from Asia, which has killed millions of native red bay trees in Florida and the SE — planting nursery stock from disease-free sources and monitoring for sudden wilting and sapwood streaking is strongly advised.

directions: Persea borbonia is a native evergreen tree of coastal plain pine flatwoods, maritime forests, and hammocks from Virginia to Texas, with aromatic, leathery, lance-shaped leaves that smell strongly of bay laurel when crushed (it is the same family as Laurus nobilis and the leaves are used as a spice substitute) and small, blue-black drupes in fall that are critical food for the palamedes swallowtail, which uses it as a larval host plant, as well as numerous songbirds and wildlife species.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade; dwarf pawpaw tolerates deeper shade than the common pawpaw (A. triloba) and is the best Asimina for understorey positions.

directions: Asimina parviflora is a native, small, understory shrub or multi-stemmed shrublet of the southeastern United States pine flatwoods and mixed forests, closely related to the common pawpaw (A. triloba) but much shorter (2–5 feet versus 15–30 feet), with smaller, maroon-purple flowers in spring and small, yellow to orange, custardy, pawpaw-like fruits 2–3 inches long in summer and early fall that are edible with the same rich, tropical-flavored flesh as the larger species. It is a larval host plant for the zebra swallowtail butterfly alongside the larger species and is a unique fruiting option for deeply shaded, woodland garden situations where conventional fruit trees cannot grow.
